Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but I'd end up scrolling through my phone or just zoning out. I learn best by listening, so I'd put on lecture recordings while doing other stuff, but I'm not sure how much actually stuck. Most of my 16 hours a week were probably spent half-focused at best. I showed up to class almost every time, but that doesn't mean my brain was fully there.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without really studying, then panic when something was due and cram everything in. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. With my extracurriculars and everything else going on, I just couldn't stick to a regular schedule even when I tried. It was exhausting trying to keep up with everything.
Q3: Usually it was either boredom or panic that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd be sitting around with nothing to do and I'd think, 'I should probably check the LMS,' even though I rarely did anything productive once I was there. Other times it was pure deadline terror—like realizing an assignment was due in a few hours and scrambling to figure out what I needed to do.
Q4: Yeah, for a while logging in became kind of automatic, like checking social media. I'd open it up, scroll through the discussion boards, maybe watch part of a video. It felt like I was being productive without actually doing the hard work. The gamified task thing they introduced made it feel a bit more interesting for a minute, but that wore off pretty quickly when I realized I still had to actually do stuff.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually kind of nice because I could just listen to what other people were saying and chime in without too much pressure. I also liked when there were audio explanations or recorded lectures because that's how I learn best. Reading long PDFs just makes my eyes glaze over, but if someone's talking me through it, I can actually follow along better.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the detailed assignment guidelines. They were so long and dense, and I just couldn't bring myself to sit through them. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The stuff that required a lot of reading or writing just felt like too much effort when I was already stressed about other things.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was quick and I could see immediate results, like getting points or badges from the gamified stuff. Or if it was something I could listen to while doing something else. Anything that felt like it would take forever or required me to write a lot just made me want to avoid it completely.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a trap, honestly. Like, if it's optional, why would I stress myself out doing it when I already can't keep up with the required stuff? Required activities at least had consequences if I didn't do them, so those got priority. But even then, I only completed like 12% of assignments, so clearly even 'required' wasn't enough motivation sometimes.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download the audio files or lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I don't have a lot of money for fancy study setups, so I made do with what I had. A lot of my studying happened while I was walking around or lying in bed with earbuds in, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q10: There were tons of times I was actually learning but it wouldn't show in the LMS. Like, I'd have a lecture playing on repeat while I was cooking or cleaning, really trying to absorb it. Or I'd be talking through concepts with friends from class in person. None of that gets tracked, but it's still studying to me.
Q11: All the time. I'd download something once, then never open the LMS version again. I'd just keep the file on my phone and go back to it whenever. It was easier than dealing with slow internet or having to log in every time. The LMS probably thought I only looked at it once, but I might've listened to it five times.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was juggling extracurriculars, and I just felt completely overwhelmed. Everything felt pointless and boring at the same time, which is a weird combination. I'd stare at the LMS and feel this wave of dread wash over me, so I'd just close it and do something else.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I have limited resources, so sometimes I was dealing with stuff at home that made it hard to focus on school. The confusion about what was actually due and when didn't help either—I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by all the tasks, and just give up.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have a video playing but I'd be thinking about a million other things. Or I'd scroll through the discussion posts without really reading them. It looked like engagement on paper, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I was physically present but mentally checked out most of the time.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important, but the anxiety made it so hard to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's not that I didn't care—I just felt paralyzed. The bigger the task seemed, the more I avoided it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd ignore stuff for weeks, then frantically log in the night before something was due. Most of my LMS activity was probably concentrated in these panic sessions. It's not a healthy way to work, but it's the only way I could get myself to actually do things.
Q17: Honestly, I didn't get much feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it sometimes made me feel worse because it reminded me how behind I was. It's hard to feel motivated by feedback when you're already drowning. I know that's on me, but it's just how it felt.
Q18: The course structure was okay, I guess, but it felt like there was always something due. The gamified elements were kind of fun at first, but they didn't change the fact that I still had to do actual work. I needed more flexibility or maybe smaller chunks of work instead of big assignments that felt impossible to start.
Q19: The big assignments made engagement really hard for me. If things had been broken into smaller, more manageable pieces, I might've done better. The audio and video content made it easier though—that matched how I learn. But the heavy reading requirements were a huge barrier that I just couldn't get past most of the time.
Q20: That's literally me. I was on the LMS a lot, clicking around, watching videos, posting in discussions. But when it came to actually submitting work, I just couldn't do it. The anxiety of turning something in and having it judged was worse than just not doing it. Plus, I'd convince myself I'd do it later and then never did.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just efficient—they get what they need and get out. Or they study offline like I do. The LMS doesn't capture everything. Someone could be studying hard from downloaded materials or textbooks and barely touch the platform but still know the content well enough to pass exams.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do while walking around. They see activity, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm genuinely interested and not just doing something because I have to. Unfortunately, I spent most of this course feeling bored and anxious, which isn't exactly engagement even when I was technically active.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before trying to start an assignment, or how I'd listen to lectures multiple times offline trying to understand. It wouldn't show that I actually cared about passing even though my actions didn't always reflect that. There's a whole emotional story behind those numbers that just doesn't get captured.
